coredata.dc:title: Improving the interfacial bonding of CNT/Cu composites using CPD bridges
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Liangqi
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Tao, Jingmei
Guo, Shengda
Tan, Songlin
coredata.dc:description: A hybrid reinforcement of carbon nanotubes and carbonized polymer dots was created and used to strengthen the copper matrix by a powder metallurgy method. The CPD-CNT/Cu composite exhibits excellent strength-ductility balance, the yield strength and tensile strength of the composites reach 195 MPa and 306 MPa, which are ∼109.5% and ∼43.5% higher than those of pure copper (93 MPa and 213 MPa), respectively; the elongation is (29%) as high as that of the copper matrix (28%). More importantly, the CNT-CPD/Cu composite exhibits a significantly higher electrical conductivity than the CNT/Cu counterpart (88.3 %IACS). The synergistic effect of the CPD and CNT is ascribed to the bridging role of the CPD in enhancing the interface between CNT and Cu matrix, which can also take full use of the electrical properties of the CNT. Our findings point to crucial tactics and areas for further research and development of copper-based composite materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanotube (CNT)
Carbonized polymer dot (CPD)
Copper matrix composites
Strengthening mechanism
Electrical conductivity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the experimental procedure.

TEM images of (a) the CPD (inset: size distribution of the CPD), (b–c) pristine CNTs and (d) pretreated CNTs. (a) Raman spectra of pristine CNTs and pretreatment of CNTs; (b) FTIR spectra of pretreatment of CNTs. Zeta potential measurement of CPD, CNT and Cu suspensions at different pH values.
SEM images of (a) as-received Cu; (b) flaked Cu powders; (c) CPD-CNT/Cu2O and (d) CPD-CNT/Cu composite powders. (e) XRD spectra of composite powders before and after reduction.

(a–c) HADDF-STEM images of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite; (c1) bright-field and (c2) dark-field TEM images; (d) EDS line scanning analysis across the nanodots; (e) TEM image recorded at the CNT/Cu near the interface area; (f) HRTEM image of the interface between the CNT and Cu matrix (inset: FFT of the white box); (g) The IFFT image of the white box marked area in Fig. 4f; (h) TEM image of the area that contains white dots; (i) HRTEM image of the white box marked area in Fig. 4h (inset: FFT of the white box); (j) IFFT image of the white box marked area in Fig. 4i.

(a) Histogram of relative density and electrical conductivity; (b) Tensile engineering stress–strain curves; (c) True stress–strain and (d) work-hardening rate curves of the sample. Fracture surface of (e–f) pure Cu; (g–h) CPD/Cu; (i–j) CNT/Cu and (k–l) CPD-CNT/Cu composites.

(a) Tensile strength of the calculated value, experimental value and strengthening contributions of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite; (b) Comparison of strength versus elongation of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite with the references; (c) Comparison of density, mechanical and electrical properties between different reinforcement composite and Cu matrix counterpart.

(a–b) TEM images of the interconnected architecture of the CPD-CNT in the Cu matrix; (c) HRTEM image of the CPD nanoparticles and CNT. (d) IFFT image of the yellow box marking area in Fig. 7c; (e) schematic diagram of the CPD and CNT in the Cu matrix. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Improving the interfacial bonding of CNT/Cu composites using CPD bridges

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Tungsten Resources High-efficiency Development and Application Technology of the Ministry of Education, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Tungsten Resources High-efficiency Development and Application Technology of the Ministry of Education
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hybrid reinforcement of carbon nanotubes and carbonized polymer dots was created and used to strengthen the copper matrix by a powder metallurgy method. The CPD-CNT/Cu composite exhibits excellent strength-ductility balance, the yield strength and tensile strength of the composites reach 195 MPa and 306 MPa, which are ∼109.5% and ∼43.5% higher than those of pure copper (93 MPa and 213 MPa), respectively; the elongation is (29%) as high as that of the copper matrix (28%). More importantly, the CNT-CPD/Cu composite exhibits a significantly higher electrical conductivity than the CNT/Cu counterpart (88.3 %IACS). The synergistic effect of the CPD and CNT is ascribed to the bridging role of the CPD in enhancing the interface between CNT and Cu matrix, which can also take full use of the electrical properties of the CNT. Our findings point to crucial tactics and areas for further research and development of copper-based composite materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of its exceptional conductivity, thermal conductivity, ductility, machinability, and corrosion resistance, copper (Cu) matrix materials are a crucial material in today's electrical and information civilization [1]. However, its heavy and soft characteristics have limited its practical application. To address these shortcomings, copper-based composite materials with excellent performance have emerged and are being responsibly developed. For instance, Cu/CNT (carbon nanotube) composites are touted to meet the growing need for Cu substitutes due to the excellent mechanical, electrical and thermal properties of CNT [2–4]. The high strength and elastic modulus of the CNT are complementary to the superior conductivity, heat conduction and ductility of the copper matrix, which makes the CNT/Cu composite possess outstanding performance in theory.

Due to issues such as CNT aggregation and poor wettability between the CNT and metal matrix, the performance of the CNT/Cu composite material is still much below the theoretical predicted value [5–7]. Meanwhile, the presence of a heterogeneous interface caused by the CNT additive has a negative impact on electrons transmission in composites, resulting in significant reduction in conductivity. Surface chemical modification method of CNT is commonly utilized to improve the dispersion [8] and precipitation of the transition phase at the interface to improve the wettability of CNTs with the matrix [9–12]. However, due to ceramic precipitates, these strategies break the microstructure of CNT, and degrading their fundamental properties, as well as affecting conduction performance. Thus, creating copper matrix composites with high mechanical and functional characteristics remains a huge challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a type of nanocarbon material, are referred to as graphene quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) according to their structural features [13]. CPD has attracted widespread attention because of its unique “core-shell” microstructure [14–16]. The surface shell of spherical CPD comprises a significant number of functional groups, giving it strong dispersibility and changeable interface properties, while the center of the sphere is distributed with a few layers of graphene on the nano-plane scale, which is beneficial to electrons and stress transfer. On this basis, CPD with this core-shell structure indicates good potential as a metal reinforcement. Unfortunately, research on its application as a reinforcement of a metal matrix is very limited.

In this study, CPD was synthesized by a bottom-up method and reinforced the copper matrix with CNT together. The purpose is to see the effect of CPD on the microstructure and macro-properties of copper composites. The CPD-CNT/Cu composite possesses a high strength-ductility balance as well as excellent electrical conduction. All of them are ascribed to the synergistic action of the CPD and CNT. Our findings provide an important technical approach and research strategy for the preparation of copper composites with good interfacial bonding and macroscopic properties.

The most common bottom-up method was adopted as follows: 6 g citric acid (CA, 99.5%, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and 1000 μL ethylenediamine (EDA) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water at the same time. This solution was transferred to the liner of the autoclave (100 mL), heated at 280 °C for 6 h, and then cooled naturally to room temperature. After dialysis and freeze-drying, CPD was obtained from the brown–black reaction solution. Meanwhile, pristine MWCNTs (Chengdu Organic Chemistry Co. Ltd., China) was impregnated in a solution of H2SO4/HNO3 (3:1 by volume) mixed acid heated at 60 °C in a water bath for 20 h. A schematic illustration of the experimental procedure for the preparation of the CPD-CNT/Cu composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

Dendritic copper powder (99.9%, 325 mesh, Shanghai Naiou Nano Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was ball milled in a stainless-steel ball mill tank with a ball-to-material ratio of 10:1 at 300 rpm for 20 h. CNTs (0.03 g) and 0.03 g CPD were placed in DI water separately and then ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. Then, ball-milled copper powder (10 g) was put into DI water and stirred for 5 min. Then, the CNT and CPD suspension was poured into the Cu powder slurry at the same time, and 40% HCl solution was slowly added dropwise to adjust the pH value of the mixed suspension to 3.0–4.0, stirred for 2 h, washed, filtered, dried and reduced at 280 °C for 6 h under a 95% N2+5% H2 atmosphere to obtain CPD-CNT/Cu2O powder. For further comparison, CNT/Cu and CPD/Cu composites with the same reinforcement content were prepared in the same way. Densification was achieved using SPS sintering at 800 °C under vacuum for 6 min at 50 MPa axial pressure in a graphite mold. The obtained composite samples have a diameter of 20 mm and a thickness of ∼3 mm. The contents of CNTs, CPD, and CPD-CNTs in the CNT/Cu, CPD/Cu, and CPD-CNT/Cu composites were all 0.6 wt%. Among them, both the CNT and CPD contents in the CPD-CNT/Cu composites were 0.3 wt%.

Detection of information indicating functional groups and the extent of defects in CNTs using infrared absorption spectroscopy (FTIR; Nicolet iS10) and Raman spectroscopy; zeta potentials of Cu, CPD and CNT suspensions at different pH values were measured by a zeta potential meter (Malvern Instrument, UK). The morphology of the raw materials, composite powders, fracture surfaces and interfacial microstructure of the composites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Nano Nova 45, Hillsboro, OR, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 TF30 S-Twin, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Furthermore, energy spectral line scans (EDS) are performed during the TEM characterization. The physical phases of the composite powders before and after reduction were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max 2400, Tokyo, Japan, Cu-Kα radiation source, λ = 1.54056 A, with an operating voltage of 40 kV and current of 30 mA), and the composite samples were slowly swept for the calculation of dislocation density. The relative density of the composite was measured using the Archimedes drainage method, the electronic conductivity of the composites was measured using a portable electronic conductivity meter (Sigma 2008B), and the mechanical properties were tested at room temperature using a universal tensiometer (AG-IS10KN) at a tensile rate of 0.2 mm/min.

Microstructures of the starting materials by TEM characterization are presented in Fig. 2 . The well-dispersed CPD has a nearly spherical structure of several nanometers. The particle statistics show an average size of ∼3.48 nm (see Fig. 2a). The original multiwalled CNTs show complete and smooth walls with a width of ∼20 nm and length of 1.0–2.0 μm (see Fig. 2b–c), whereas a number of defective sites are found on the outer wall of the CNTs after chemical etching (see Fig. 2d). These defect sites have high specific surface energy and are usually used as adsorption and nucleation sites of metal particles [17,18]. In addition, the surface of pretreated CNTs has a large number of hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., –COOH, etc.) that can improve its dispersibility [19].

The relative degree of structural disorder in CNTs can be judged by the ID/IG intensity ratio between the disorder-induced D-band and the stretching vibrations of the G-band induced by the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [20]. Fig. 2e shows that the ID/IG ratios for the pristine CNTs and pretreated CNTs were 0.53 and 0.78, respectively. This significant increase in ID/IG indicates a significant increase in the number of CNT surface defects, which is consistent with the surface morphology observed by TEM. Infrared absorption spectroscopy is commonly used to identify the functional groups on the surface of CNTs. The FTIR spectrum of the pretreated CNTs shows the presence of O–H (∼3420 cm−1 and∼ 1405 cm−1), CO (∼1765 cm−1), C–H (∼2918 cm−1) and CC (∼1630 cm−1) (see Fig. 2f). Fig. 2g gives the zeta potential values of Cu, CNTs and CPD at different pH conditions. When the pH value is adjusted to 3.0–4.0, the potential difference between the negatively charged CPD and CNTs and the positively charged Cu powder is the largest, which means strong adsorption between the components in the mixture. Our previous work has proven that CNTs and Cu can be tightly combined via electrostatic adsorption and will not separate even after ultrasonic treatment, suggesting strong and irreversible bonding between them.

The micromorphology of the as-received Cu powder and after HEBM (high-energy ball milling) are shown in Fig. 3 a–b. The dendritic electrolytic Cu powders are transformed into a flaky copper powder with a large diameter to thickness ratio after HEBM, which has a high specific surface volume for compatibility with CNTs and CPD in terms of surface contact area and geometrical properties. On the other hand, HEBM can increase the activity and surface energy of the flake powder, lower the chemical reaction barriers [21] and make it easier to bind CNTs and CPD in later electrostatic adsorption processes.

After electrostatic adsorption, the composite powder shows that CNTs are successfully adsorbed on the Cu powder, and the CNTs are covered with cube-like in situ cuprous oxide particles (see Fig. 3c), which indicates that it is difficult to further agglomerate this individual CNT. At the same time, it was found that the exposed part of the CNTs became a bridge between the cuprous oxide particles (Fig. 3c inset). This infers a close bounding relationship between the CNTs and the Cu matrix, not just surface adsorption. The cube-like in situ cuprous oxide particles were reduced to copper completely after hydrogen reduction, which is in good agreement with the XRD spectra. The surface of the reduced composite powder becomes flat and smooth, and the CNTs are still basically coated inside the copper matrix, which means that a higher density and good payload transfer effect will be obtained (see Fig. 3d–e).

Fig. 4 (a-b) shows the HADDF-TEM image of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite. A number of annealing twins and dislocation entanglement can be found. Since the face-centered cubic structure of copper has a lower stacking fault energy, annealing twins are easily formed during reduction or sintering, and the twin boundary can effectively hinder dislocation movement and can also be used as a favorable location for storing dislocations to improve the blocking ability of dislocations [28]. Macroscopically, the uniform dispersion of the hard reinforcement phase in the matrix can hinder the propagation of microcracks. Therefore, the work hardening rate can be greatly promoted, thus improving the uniform elongation rate of the composites. The high-angle annular dark-field (HADDF)-TEM image of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite displays some holes (marked with white arrows), which are most likely caused by ion beam piercing at the weaker areas during ion thinning, and some nanodots, which are formed by clusters of the CPD (see Fig. 4c). Fig. 4c1-c2 show the bright-field and dark-field TEM images of the nanodots, respectively. These nanodots are uniformly distributed not only on the grain boundaries but also inside the grains after careful observation. EDS line scanning analysis across the nanodots gives the opposite signal trends of C and Cu elements, the convex peaks of C and the pit peaks of Cu appear at points 1 and 2, implying CPD gathering at these nanodots. This explains why holes are formed due to the larger CPD clusters being easily destroyed by ion-beam bombardment (see Fig. 4d).

Fig. 4e shows the TEM image of the CNT/Cu interface area. Based on the atomic number contrast principle, the white areas can be identified as CNTs, and the dark areas can be identified as the copper matrix. At the same time, the linear structure of the CNTs can be observed in the bright region. Fig. 4f gives the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the interface between the CNT and Cu matrix marked with the black box in Fig. 4e. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image in the lower-left inset shows the diffraction spots of the white box marked area, and the diffraction spots of Cu (200) and (311) can be observed. The included angle between the diffraction spots is ∼25°, which is consistent with the calculation result by the formula of the included angle of the cubic crystal system. Not surprisingly, diffraction spots of Cu2O (200) are also observed due to the intermediate product of copper oxide nanocrystals between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CNT and the copper atoms during the sintering process [22]. These nanocrystals tend to form at the defect sites of the CNT, leading to the C–Cu2O/Cu transition interface, which is beneficial to the connection between the CNT and Cu matrix [23]. In view of the FFT and inverse FFT (IFFT) patterns of the marked areas in Fig. 4g, the interplanar spacings of these lattice fringes are measured to be ∼0.181 nm, ∼0.109 nm and ∼0.214 nm, corresponding to the d-spacings of the Cu (200), (311) and Cu2O (200) planes, respectively. Meanwhile, a few dislocations found in the Cu matrix near the interfaces (“T”-shaped symbols in the IFFT images of Fig. 4g) are likely to originate from the noticeable internal stress caused by the mismatch of the thermal expansion coefficient (CTE) between the CNT and Cu matrix [24]. These misfit dislocations are conducive to the promotion of interface adhesion [25]. Fig. 4h shows the TEM image of the area containing the white nanodots. An amorphous carbon network and crystalline CPD nanoparticles are discovered, which may be formed owing to the aggregation of the CPD clusters. Fig. 4i shows the HRTEM image of the white box marked area in Fig. 4h. In the lower-left inset, the FFT image shows the diffraction spots in the white-box marked area i, the diffraction spots of C (111) (200) and (311) can be observed, and IFFT patterns of the marked area j are shown in Fig. 4j, the interplanar spacings of these lattice fringes are measured to be ∼0.206 nm, ∼0.178 nm and ∼0.107 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of C (111) (200) and (311) planes, respectively. As an excellent electron acceptor and donor, uniformly distributed CPD can act as a fast channel for electron transport, effectively reducing electron scattering at the grain boundaries.

Fig. 5 a shows the histogram of the relative density and electrical conductivity of the pure Cu and the composite samples. The density of the samples is roughly the same, but the electrical conductivity is quite different; those of the pure Cu, CPD/Cu, CNT/Cu and CPD-CNT/Cu composites are 92.40, 95.50, 85.06 and 88.33%IACS, respectively. As mentioned above, well-distributed CPD has a significant improvement effect on the conductivity of the composites.

Fig. 5b shows the tensile engineering stress–strain curves of the samples. Each strength value represents the average result of four measurements. The specific dimensions of the tensile specimen are shown in the inset of Fig. 5b. The CPD/Cu composite shows a Yield Strength (YS) of 99.60 (±3.6) MPa and Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of 221.74 (±6.1) MPa, which are merely 6.79% and 3.79% higher than that of pure Cu; actually, the YS and UTS are considerably increased to 170.25 (±5.7) MPa and 269.29 (±7.6) MPa for the CNT/Cu composite, corresponding to 82.53% and 26.30% improvements, respectively; the YS and UTS values of the CPD-CNT/Cu composites are 195.36 (±4.2) MPa and 305.95 (±8.1) MPa, corresponding to 109.46% and 43.50% enhancements, respectively.

The true stress–strain and work-hardening rate curves of the pure Cu and composites are displayed in Fig. 5c and d, respectively. Obviously, the work hardening rate of the samples corresponds to an initial steep decrease up to a true strain of ∼2%, followed by a slow and gradual decrease with further increasing true strain. Meanwhile, a prolonged positive work-hardening rate will lead to a large true strain (the absence of work hardening causes localized deformation leading to low ductility) [26], and remarkable positive work hardening is critical for obtaining a large uniform elongation to avoid premature strain localization, such as necking [27]. It is noteworthy that the work hardening rate of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite is significantly higher than those of the other counterparts.

The fracture morphology of the pure Cu and composites is provided in Fig. 5. Dimples are present on the fracture surfaces of all samples, indicating a typical plastic fracture, which is in line with the result of the tensile curves. In particular, the dimples (see Fig. 5e and f) on the fracture surface of pure Cu are uniform and complete, whereas the dimples of the CPD/Cu composite are relatively deeper, inferring that it has preferable plasticity and elongation (see Fig. 5g and h). This is also consistent with the results of the CPD in improving the work hardening ability. Fig. 5i and j shows that CNT clusters can be observed with some remaining pores on the fractured surface of the CNT/Cu composite, which will act as crack sources to reduce the elongation during the tensile process. From the enlarged fracture morphology of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite (see Fig. 5k and l), CNTs are uniformly dispersed in the Cu matrix. Notably, the inset shows that the CNT is fractured after the tensile test, which can further justify the strong bonding provided by the strengthening phase, demonstrates that good interfacial bonding allows for effective load transfer and significant enhancement in the strength of the composite.

Understanding the underlying reinforcing mechanism makes it easier to understand the strengthening behavior of composite materials. In this study, a comparison of the Cu matrix and CPD-CNT/Cu composite is carried out. According to the related literature and described microstructures, the enhanced strength of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite should have various probable strengthening processes simultaneously: (i) grain refining (Δ
GR ), (ii) load transfer (Δ
LT ), (iii) dislocation strengthening (Δ
D ) [29]. The many reinforcing processes caused by the reinforcements can be investigated as expressed in Eq. (1).
(1)
σ c
=
σ m
+ Δ
σ
G R
+ Δ
σ
L T
+ Δ
σ D
where
σ m
(213.21 MPa) is the UTS of the matrix;
σ c
is the UTS of composite; and
Δ
σ
G R
,
Δ
σ
L T
and
Δ
σ D
are the strengthening contributions from grain refinement, load transfer and dislocation strengthening, respectively. Their calculation values are 17.12, 41.97 and 31.39 MPa (the specific calculation process is described in SI). Fig. 6 a summarizes the estimated calculations and experimental values of the strengthening contributions. The estimated values from the strengthening mechanisms are quite comparable to the experimental values, proving the existence and rationality of these strengthening mechanisms in the CPD-CNT/Cu composite. Both load-transfer and dislocation have an important strengthening effects in the composites. At the same time, we discover that the CPD-CNT/Cu composite material has an elongation close to that of pure copper. Fig. 6b shows the elongation and tensile strength results of Cu matrix composites reinforced by nanomaterials from other works [30–40]. A perfect equilibrium between tensile strength and elongation is achieved in our study.

Fig. 6c gives the comparison of density, mechanical and electrical properties between different reinforcement composites and Cu matrix counterpart. It can be seen that when the relative densities of all samples are close with each other (at the range of 96.7–97.6%), the increment in the strength of the CPD/Cu is small, but the elongation and conductivity increase significantly; on the contrary, the strength of the CNT/Cu increases obviously, but the elongation and conductivity have dropped severely. CNT-CPD/Cu combines the advantages of them, realizing the complementarity of CPD and CNT performance advantages, while also having excellent mechanical and electrical properties.

The better strength-ductility-conductivity balance of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite is attributable to the synergistic action of the CPD and CNT reinforcements, since the enhancement is much higher even than the sum of CPD and CNT applied individually. Our prepared CPD, which is made from CA and EDA, the condensation polymer is further crosslinked, resulting in the inner polymer core with network structure. Actually, the carbon core of the CPD include several subcategories: complete carbonized cores, the para-crystalline carbon structure composed of tiny carbon clusters with polymer frameworks, as well as highly dehydrated crosslinking and curling polymer framework [41]. These CPD will keep reacting and crosslinking, generating a larger cluster network (10–20 nm), which we call the “CPD cluster” [42].

Furthermore, these CPD clusters are easy to attach to the surfaces of CNT due to their abundance of functional groups (see Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7a shows that the CNT may be detected in the boundaries of many copper grains. Since the length of the CNT is in the micrometer range, CPD cannot be identified. However, after further magnification, a large number of CPDs are displayed on the CNT. It is worth mentioning that due to the existence of the CPD, the interface between CNT and Cu becomes very tight, as marked by the red box in Fig. 7b. HRTEM and IFFT images prove the microstructure of the CNT and CPD (Fig. 7c and d). The strong connection between the CNT and CPD is due to chemical absorption between the carboxyl and amino functional group interactions. There are a large number of –COOH groups on the acidified CNT surface and a sea of –NH2 groups on the surface of the CPD, resulting in the CPD self-assembles onto the CNT. Chemically adsorbed CPD will pass on its properties to CNT, such as easy dispersion and hydrophilicity.

A schematic diagram based on the microstructure characteristic is constructed in Fig. 7e to help comprehend the structure relationship. In contrast to the clean interface between CNT and Cu, a high number of functional group combinations are formed at their interface due to the presence of CPD. Owing to the strong surface reactivity of the CPD, when the CPD concentration rises, the contact between the CPD and Cu gets stronger and tighter. CPD and CNT were introduced to our preparation procedure, respectively. Hence, the three types of interfaces mentioned in Fig. 7e are all present in the composite at the same time. The bonding degree rises as the order of CNT, CNT-CPD and CPD increases. We can reasonably infer that the emergence of the CPD greatly contributes to the full exploitation of CNT performance. By combining the contribution values of the various mechanisms mentioned above, we also can find that adding CPD will significantly increase the contribution of load transfer and dislocation strengthening, implying that the CPD not only increases the load transfer efficiency but also plays a very critical role in pinning the movement of dislocations. In addition, the activated carbon atoms created by the decomposition non-core section of the CPD at high temperature will also purify the Cu grain surface, especially the oxygen atoms adsorbed on the Cu surface will react with the activated carbon atoms and be removed.

Reported researches indicate that it is difficult even impossible to have high strength and high conductivity simultaneously for a copper-based composite [43]. The main reason is that the improvement of the electromechanical mechanism is contradictory [44]. For example, to improve the mechanical strength of composite materials, it is always to refine the Cu grains or introduces a second phase to improve the Cu/C interface bonding and load transfer capacity. However, with the increase in the number of grain boundaries and the introduction of second phases, electrons will be scattered intensively, and the conductivity will be decreased accordingly. However, in our study, the conductivity of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite is much better than that of the CNT/Cu counterpart, and the conductivity of the CPD/Cu composite even exceeds that of pure copper. Thus, we believe the CPD as a new nanomaterial with unique electrical properties, plays an important role in promoting the movement or transfer of electrons.

According to Matthiessen's law [45], the resistance is mainly caused by the interaction of the interface scattering, boundary scattering, dislocation and phonon scattering without considering of the influence of impurities. The resistance of a material can be expressed as Eq. (1):
(1)
R =
R
m c
+
R
i n t
+
R
G B
+
R
d i s
+
R
p h o
where R is the resistance of material, R
mc is the resistance of single crystal material, R
int is the resistance caused by interface scattering, R
GB is the resistance caused by grain boundary scattering, R
dis is the resistance caused by dislocation scattering, and R
pho
R
p h o
is the resistance caused by phonon scattering. In Eq. (1), phonon scattering is an inherent characteristic of the material, which is related with the temperature, here, its influence on the total resistance can be ignored at the room temperature. Besides, we always use the same copper powder raw material in the experiment, so the influence of this part on the resistance can also be ignored. Hence, compared with the Cu matrix, the change of the resistance value is mainly caused by interface scattering ΔR
int , grain boundary scattering ΔR
GB , and dislocation scattering ΔR
dis , which can be described as follows:
(2)
Δ R = Δ
R
i n t
+ Δ
R
G B
+ Δ
R
d i s

Combine the microstructure analysis of our above discussion, the grain boundary and dislocation of the composite is significantly increased compared with those of the matrix. Therefore, ΔR
GB and ΔR
dis of the CPD-CNT/Cu composite are increased obviously. However, the conductivity means ΔR is decreased, which means ΔR < = 0. Therefore, we can reasonably infer that the interface scattering in the composite material is greatly reduced compared to the matrix. Moreover, its negative contribution to resistance exceeds the sum of the positive contributions of the grain boundary and dislocation scattering.

The core of CPD is composed of the N doped nano-graphene sheets. Actually, this graphene zone has very high carrier mobility due to the conjugated π electrons [46]. In additional, as a one-dimensional ballistic conductor, there is no scattering of electrons in the CNT. Hence, in the composite, the electron scattering caused by grain boundaries and dislocations can be reduced heavily by increasing electron paths or channels of the CPD and CNT. From Fig. 4f, CPD does not exist alone in the form of dots, but a carbon network inserted with countless carbon dots. When these networks are closely located on the copper grain boundaries, they can be regarded as bridges for free electron migration (see Fig. 7e). According to the differential equation of Ohm's law derived from classical electron theory, the conductivity of materials can be expressed as Eq. (3):
(3)
γ =
n
e 2
λ ‾
v ‾
m
where γ is electron conductivity,
n
,
e
and m represent the number, charge and mass of electrons, respectively.
λ ‾
is mean free path,
v ‾
is average rate of electrons. The conductivity of the material is proportional to the mean free path. The mean free path of CNT (2.5 × 104 nm [47]) is reported to be substantially greater than that of Cu (40 nm [48]). It can be said that the amount of electron transfer per unit time is related to the proportion of copper involved in electron transport. Here, it can be seen that the CPD acts as a bridge role in the process of electron transfer from Cu to CNT, and even from CNT to CNT, which will undoubtedly effectively improve the conductivity of the composite material by making full use of the electrical properties of the CNT.

This study provides new ideas and experimental evidence for the preparation of copper matrix composites employing powder metallurgy with excellent mechanical and electrical properties at the same time. CPD-CNT/Cu composites combine excellent combination strength, toughness, and electricity. The well-distributed CPD has a significant improvement effect on the conductivity of the composites. The reinforcement is attributed to the synergistic effect of CPD and CNT, which involves good interfacial bonding between the CPD-CNT hybrids and the Cu matrix. The CPD forms a fast channel for electronic transmission at the copper grain boundaries and dislocation, which boost the electron transfer dramatically.

Liangqi Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Rui Bao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jianhong Yi: Supervision. Jingmei Tao: Validation. Shengda Guo: Data curation. Songlin Tan: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345) and the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202102AG050006, No. 202002AB080001). Foundation of Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province (2020KF004), the independent project of Jiangxi advanced Copper Industry Research Institute (ZL-202006).

ce:section.ce:para:
